data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was epiphenomenal to a hyperconnectivity crisis in an early 'globalized' trade network that amplified local shocks into systemic failure","mechanism": "The Uluburun shipwreck model of interdependence created systemic coupling: Aegean silver, Anatolian copper, Levantine grain, and Cypriot copper were nodally specialized; a shock in any region propagated through debt-bond relations ( documented in Ugarit archives ) causing simultaneous insolvency cascades, while Egypt's semi-autarkic grain production decoupled it from network failure","scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "Cyprus"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Mesopotamia"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Ugarit archives show no debt-crisis correspondence between regions contemporaneous with collapse", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Material culture shows regional autarky rather than specialization in the decades before collapse", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egypt was equally trade-dependent on Levantine timber and incense, and records show no substitution or domestic provision", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Atypical network-systems candidate applying modern financial-crisis analogies to pre-monetary exchange; deliberately provocative anachronism."}
